patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/2] btrfs: fix false IO failure after falling back to buffered IO
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a false IO failure issue in Btrfs after falling back to buffered IO. When direct_write() encounters an EFAULT error from btrfs_dio_write(), it should retry the fault instead of immediately switching to buffered IO. The patch introduces a new logic to handle this scenario, attempting to fault in as many remaining pages as possible and retrying if progress is made. If no progress is made, it falls back to buffered IO. This change aims to prevent infinite loops that could occur when writing to a file with an mmap-ed range.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the patch incorrectly returns -EFAULT when btrfs_direct_write() has not submitted any bytes for a dio structure, causing iomap_dio_complete() to return 0 and leading to a false IO failure. The reviewer is skeptical of this fix and suggests re-examining the original solution of doing OE split and submitting separate direct writes.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the page fault in will not address the issue because the page cache is invalidated, and suggested re-examining the original fix of doing an OE split and submitting separate direct writes.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed skepticism about the patch's fix for false IO failures after falling back to buffered IO, suggesting that it masks the failure and is not detecting it in some cases, such as nodatasum mount option. They also noted that btrfs_dio_write() should initially fail with -EFAULT but no longer does.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed skepticism about the proposed patch to handle write buffer faults by falling back to buffered IO, suggesting that a previous approach of doing OE split and submitting separate direct writes was more effective.
review_comments.raw_body:
It doesn't return -EFAULT.

btrfs_direct_write() returned an dio pointer, although it has not 
submitted any bytes for that dio structure.

So later iomap_dio_complete() returned 0.

The full trace printk looks like this:

  dio-append-buf--2501    [008] .....   627.444860: btrfs_direct_write: 
enter, root=5 ino=257 pos=0 iov_count=4096
  dio-append-buf--2501    [008] .....   627.444905: btrfs_direct_write: 
dio complete, ret=0
  dio-append-buf--2501    [008] .....   627.444906: btrfs_direct_write: 
buffered, pos=0 iov_counter=4096
  dio-append-buf--2501    [008] .....   627.444935: btrfs_direct_write: 
exit, root=5 ino=257 pos=0 iov_count=4096 ret=-5 written=0

---

Furthermore, the page fault in won't make any difference for this 
particular case, exactly explained by the comment itself, that the page 
cache will be invalidated.

---

The buffered fallback for datacsum is masking the failure for most 
cases, and the test case itself is also hiding the failure even for 
nodatasum mount option, so we are not detecting it for a long time.

Secondly I also believed the btrfs_dio_write() should fail with -EFAULT 
initially, but that's no longer the case.

My guess is iomap dio error handling change, but will need extra digging 
to pin down the iomap commit.

---

That's also the test case I'm failing with IOMAP_DIO_BOUNCE, but I think 
we should fix the g/362 failure first, as it is already failing now.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: use shifts for sectorsize and nodesize
patches_acked.patch_summary: The patch proposes using shifts to calculate sectorsize and nodesize in btrfs.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Raised a question about whether it's nicer to add 1 to the shift operand instead of using shifts directly.

review_comments.author: David Sterba
review_comments.summary: Provided a comment from the diff context to support the use of shifts, but did not explicitly approve or object to the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 09:04:09AM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> > --- a/fs/btrfs/relocation.c
> > +++ b/fs/btrfs/relocation.c
> > @@ -1560,7 +1560,7 @@ static noinline_for_stack int merge_reloc_root(struct reloc_control *rc,
> >  	 * and * 2 since we have two trees to COW.
> >  	 */
> >  	reserve_level = max_t(int, 1, btrfs_root_level(root_item));
> > -	min_reserved = fs_info->nodesize * reserve_level * 2;
> > +	min_reserved = (reserve_level << fs_info->nodesize_bits) * 2;
> 
> Is it nicer to just add 1 to the shift operand?

There's fragment of the comment in the diff context, in full

        /*
         * In merge_reloc_root(), we modify the upper level pointer to swap the
         * tree blocks between reloc tree and subvolume tree.  Thus for tree
         * block COW, we COW at most from level 1 to root level for each tree.
         *
         * Thus the needed metadata size is at most root_level * nodesize,
         * and * 2 since we have two trees to COW.
         */

so the "* 2" matches it.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: fix deadlock cloning inline extent when using flushoncommit

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations
[PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where the defrag_mode in the Linux kernel's page allocator fails to handle non-reclaimable allocations, such as those made with GFP_ATOMIC flags. When defrag_mode is enabled, it enforces ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT to prevent fragmentation, but this causes failures for non-reclaimable allocations that cannot invoke direct reclaim or compaction. The patch clears the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag and retries the allocation if it requests kswapd reclaim but cannot do direct reclaim itself. This fix addresses a large number of SLUB allocation failures under network-heavy workloads.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that defrag_mode=1 causes SLUB allocation failures for GFP_ATOMIC allocations under network-heavy workloads, agreeing that the issue is significant enough to warrant a fix.
review_comments.raw_body:
We observed it on a few of the Meta workloads that adopted
defrag_mode=1.

For the service under load there were 85509 SLUB allocation failures
messages in dmesg within 2 hours. All of them are GFP_ATOMIC allocations
for skbuff_head_cache, despite free pages being available in other
migratetype freelists (~13 GB free).

Since it is networking path from the practical point of view, this means
dropped packets, failed RPC requests, tail latency spikes and overall
service degradation.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the urgency of the patch, suggesting it may not be necessary to include it in the upcoming kernel release (v7.1) due to its relatively low failure rate.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK, thanks.   I assume 12 failures per second isn't a disaster, and that
there's no need to fast-track this into 7.1?

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Vlastimil (SUSE) requested more information about the usage and motivations behind defrag_mode, specifically asking for insights on which workloads benefit from it and what are the expectations.
review_comments.raw_body:
Do you (or Johannes) have some observations to share about what
motivated those to adopt it, what kind of workloads benefit and how?
Because I have no idea who uses this mode and what are the expectations.

Thanks,
Vlastimil

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that defrag_mode was originally developed to address higher order / THP success rates under pressure, but its implementation has structural issues, specifically that it relies on preproducing watermark buffers in contiguous pageblocks for higher orders, which can fail under competition. The reviewer expressed hope to make defrag_mode the default eventually and acknowledged some bugs and overreclaim issues still being figured out.
review_comments.raw_body:
As you may remember it was developed to help with higher order / THP
success rates under pressure.

The impetus for actually deploying it was that we saw issues with
avalanches of large page cache folios vacuuming up the higher-order
chunks; this (ironically) also led to failures on the network side.

It's kind of a structural problem. We have real preproduction buffers
for order-0 pages through the watermarks. But for higher orders we
only ensure there is at least one page. That easily fails under even
mild competition.

Since we wanted to roll defrag_mode for THP in multi-tenant systems
anyway, we figured we might as well take the plunge now and battle
test the feature this way.

defrag_mode fixes *that* issue, by preproducing watermark buffers in
contiguous pageblocks - making everything up to that order more
readily available. I'm still hoping to make it the default eventually,
which was the plan with the original huge page allocator series. As we
keep leaning into higher order requests more and more, and especially
grow the non-optional ones, we kind of need non-optional preproduction
guarantees for higher orders as well.

But there are bugs like this one, and we're still figuring out some
overreclaim issues with it in production as well. So I'm glad it's
optional for the time being ;-)

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()
[PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch annotates guard constructors in the Linux kernel with __nonnull(), which tells the compiler to warn when a NULL pointer is statically known to be passed. This is done by re-declaring the constructor with __nonnull() for DEFINE_GUARD() and directly annotating it for __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(). The __nonnull() macro is defined in compiler_attributes.h, following existing conventions. This change aims to improve code quality by catching potential NULL pointer issues at compile-time.

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra noted that the build robot reported an issue with clang-23 being confused by the patch, and suggested that Dmitry Ilvokhin is likely CC'd on the email.
review_comments.raw_body:
The build robot found something to hate in this one. I think you're on
Cc there. It looks to me like clang-23 is confused somehow, but who
knows.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a conflict between their __nonnull() macro and glibc's own macro, proposed three possible solutions: dropping the macro, renaming it to avoid conflict, or redefining it to match glibc's convention; they are leaning towards option 1.
review_comments.raw_body:
Seems like clang is not confused here, I was able to reproduce the problem
with GCC 11 as well.

There is a conflict with glibc's own __nonnull macro

    https://elixir.bootlin.com/glibc/glibc-2.43/source/misc/sys/cdefs.h#L560-L562

which doesn't match the one from include/linux/compiler_attributes.h.
They usually don't collide, except for User Mode Linux builds, which
include both kernel and userspace headers.

Options are:

1. Drop the __nonnull() macro from include/linux/compiler_attributes.h
   and use __attribute__((__nonnull__())) directly in
   include/linux/cleanup.h. This is a bit unfortunate, since __nonnull()
   seems like a useful shortcut, but seems like the simplest solution.

2. Rename __nonnull() to __nonnull_args() to avoid the conflict.
   A returns_nonnull attribute is supported by compilers, so the name
   fits, but it diverges from the existing naming convention in
   include/linux/compiler_attributes.h.

3. #define __nonnull(params) __attribute__((__nonnull__ params)). This
   keeps the name and is compatible with both kernel and glibc usage.
   Current call sites use __nonnull() with no arguments, which works
   identically. Future callers with specific parameter numbers would use
   __nonnull((1, 2)) with double parens, matching glibc's convention. I
   don't like this option, listed it here for the sake of completeness.

I am leaning towards option 1 so far.

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miguel Ojeda suggested standardizing the spelling of __nonnull() across the kernel by introducing a single, consistent naming convention for this attribute, such as just 'nonnull', '__knonnull', or other variations, to avoid confusion and make maintenance easier.
review_comments.raw_body:
:(

What about other similar names? i.e. a variation of your option 2,
e.g. just `nonnull` (we also have others like that, i.e. no
underscore, e.g. `noinline`), or `___nonnull` (triple underscore, but
may be confusing), or a suffix/prefix letter, e.g. `__knonnull` (for
kernel nonnull)...

i.e. it would be nice to have a "standard" spelling for ourselves, and
also replace the existing `__attribute__((nonnull))`s we have
elsewhere in the tree.

Cheers,
Miguel

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the macro name might be a concern and is considering alternative names such as __knonnull() or __nonnull_args(), but prefers the latter due to its descriptive nature.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, a different name might work as well. The main question is which
name to pick.

Plain nonnull() is a bit dangerous. It might collide with existing
identifiers (now or in the future) and is not great for a kernel-wide
macro. I think we got away with plain noinline, because it was there
forever. There are also at least 24 nonnull attribute usages in the
kernel that need to be converted atomically. This can be done, but it
increases the scope of the patchset, which I'd rather avoid.

___nonnull() with triple underscore might be a bit confusing, I agree.

__knonnull() might work. I like __nonnull_args() better: it gives the
reader a hint about the semantics, while __knonnull() is just a
collision-avoidance trick without conveying any additional meaning. That
being said, I can totally do __knonnull().

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 4/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards
[PATCH v4 0/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks from unconditional guards in the Linux kernel. The checks were dead code because the guards guarantee a non-NULL lock pointer at destructor time. The patch affects several macros, including DEFINE_GUARD(), DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(), and DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(). It also fixes a few specific instances where the NULL check was not being used correctly. The changes are verified to have no performance impact, with a negligible reduction in code size.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression
[PATCH v4 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch moves a NULL check from the __DEFINE_UNLOCK_GUARD macro to the call site of irqdesc_lock's unlock expression, making the handling of NULL locks explicit and preparing for future removal of the NULL check from the macro.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class
[PATCH v4 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch converts the nvdimm_bus guard from using DEFINE_GUARD() to a combination of DEFINE_CLASS() and DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(). This change is necessary because the upcoming removal of NULL checks in DEFINE_GUARD() constructors would cause a compile error for nvdimm_bus, which can pass NULL. The conversion does not introduce any functional changes.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v6 5/7] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to qspinlock
Re: [PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/migrate: find_mm_struct: fix race between security checks and suid exec

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR padding calculation in ff_encode_getdeviceinfo

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: fix XDR padding calculation in ff_encode_getdeviceinfo

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server where the XDR padding calculation for the da_addr_body field is incorrect, leading to stale reply-page content being leaked to the client and mis-alignment of subsequent version list decodes. The fix uses xdr_align_size() to match the actual encoded data written by xdr_encode_opaque().

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the unaligned pattern in nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget() and suggested converting to xdrgen instead, but noted that this would be a bigger and less back-portable change.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 27, 2026, at 2:30 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_ff_encode_getdeviceinfo() computes the da_addr_body reservation
> as 16 + netid_len + addr_len, but the subsequent xdr_encode_opaque()
> calls emit 8 + round_up(netid_len, 4) + round_up(addr_len, 4) bytes.
> The mismatch means the declared da_addr_body length exceeds the actual
> encoded data by 2-8 bytes on every flexfile GETDEVICEINFO reply,
> leaking stale reply-page content to the client and mis-aligning the
> subsequent version list decode.
>
> Use xdr_align_size() for each string length to match what
> xdr_encode_opaque() actually writes.
>
> Fixes: efcae97fa425 ("NFSD: da_addr_body field missing in some 
> GETDEVICEINFO replies")
> Assisted-by: kres:claude-opus-4-6
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c | 3 ++-
>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c b/fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c
> index f9f7e38cba13..7f357dbd1bb1 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c
> @@ -94,7 +94,8 @@ nfsd4_ff_encode_getdeviceinfo(struct xdr_stream *xdr,
>  	}
> 
>  	/* len + padding for two strings */
> -	addr_len = 16 + da->netaddr.netid_len + da->netaddr.addr_len;
> +	addr_len = 8 + xdr_align_size(da->netaddr.netid_len) +
> +		   xdr_align_size(da->netaddr.addr_len);
>  	ver_len = 20;
> 
>  	len = 4 + ver_len + 4 + addr_len;
>
> ---
> base-commit: b69fc3eaa867d0caa904634ea7a1b4569411b163
> change-id: 20260527-pnfs-fixes-23451bb03d57
>
> Best regards,
> -- 
> Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

Fixes tag might/should be 9b9960a0ca47 ? LLM reviews vary on this.

nfsd4_ff_encode_layoutget at fs/nfsd/flexfilelayoutxdr.c:40-41 has the
same unaligned pattern for UID/GID strings: 8 + uid.len + 8 + gid.len.
Do you intend to address that calculation as well?

My preference here is to convert to xdrgen instead, but that's a much
bigger and less back-portable change.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: block non-SAVEFH ops after FOREIGN PUTFH to prevent NULL deref

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: block non-SAVEFH ops after FOREIGN PUTFH to prevent NULL deref

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential NULL pointer dereference in the nfsd kthread when handling foreign filehandles (FHs) and inter-Server Server Copy (SSC) operations. The issue arises when a remote client crafts a COMPOUND request that includes an inter-SSC COPY with additional ops inserted between the source PUTFH and SAVEFH, causing fh_export to be NULL. To fix this, the patch introduces a gate in the dispatch loop that blocks non-SAVEFH ops after FOREIGN PUTFH if fh_dentry is NULL.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified the potential issue with NULL pointer dereference when handling foreign filehandles and inter-SSC operations. Approved the patch for application to nfsd-testing.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Wed, 27 May 2026 10:53:37 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> When CONFIG_NFSD_V4_2_INTER_SSC is enabled, nfsd4_putfh() can return
> success with fh_dentry and fh_export both NULL if fh_verify() returns
> nfserr_stale and putfh->no_verify is true. The NFSD4_FH_FOREIGN flag
> is set, but the compound dispatch loop only uses this flag to bypass
> the nfserr_nofilehandle check -- it does not prevent subsequent ops
> from running with a NULL fh_dentry.
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] nfsd: block non-SAVEFH ops after FOREIGN PUTFH to prevent NULL deref
      commit: d8db9ced22a876091c432c4adb35e5d05be196aa

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

review_comments.author: Baokun Li

Hi Joanne,

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Notified the reviewer of a newer version of the patch series and offered to add their Reviewed-bys to the updated series.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [BUG] iomap: NULL dereference in iomap_finish_folio_read after deferred failed read
Re: [PATCH] fuse: disable default bdi strictlimiting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch disables default strictlimiting in the fuse (Filesystem in Userspace) module to prevent performance regressions caused by excessive write throttling due to large folio sizes and limited dirty budgets. The current implementation can lead to severe performance issues, including oscillating writes between freerunning and full throttling, especially on systems with limited RAM or cgroups with memory constraints. By disabling strictlimiting, the patch aims to prevent misbehaving servers from accumulating too many dirty pages, which can degrade write throughput for other filesystems.

review_comments.author: Horst Birthelmer
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Horst Birthelmer agrees with Joanne Koong's concerns about large folio sizes causing excessive throttling and performance regressions, stating that they saw similar issues when enabling large folios for testing.

Reviewer Horst Birthelmer expressed uncertainty about the best approach to handle bdi strictlimiting, suggesting that a module parameter might be a suitable solution to balance the need for limits on unprivileged servers while avoiding unnecessary restrictions.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sounds good to me, since we have seen pretty much the same when we enabled
large folios for testing.

---

Here I'm not really sure what the most logical and sane way would be.
I really don't like limits for no reason but I understand the necessity
to have limits enabled for unpriviledged servers.

Do you think a module parameter is the right way to go here?
The connection parameter might be a problem since an admin would have
to set it for a large number of mounts.

Horst

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the need for implementing fuse strictlimiting as a kernel module parameter, suggesting that userspace could easily handle it through libfuse and/or mount helper.
review_comments.raw_body:
Not saying no to a module option, but I am curious.
This admin opt-in per mount could easily be implemented in userspace
in either libfuse and/or mount helper.

For such a niche ("I know what I am doing") feature, why does it matter
to do this in the kernel module params?

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer suggested that the folio alloc logic could modulate the folio size based on how close the system is to the dirty threshold, proposing a dynamic approach to prevent excessive throttling.

Reviewer noted that tracking partial dirty won't help free up large folios unless the dirty part is migrated to a smaller one, and questioned whether adding this complexity is worthwhile.

Reviewer noted that strictlimiting can be disabled via the /sys/class/bdi interface, and suggested introducing a config/module option to make this off-by-default behavior possible.
sentiment_signals: no clear disagreement or request for change
review_comments.raw_body:
Some handwaving: would it be possible for the folio alloc logic to
modulate the folio size based on how close are we to the dirty
threshold?

E.g. if the number of folios to reach the dirty limit is less than 10,
then start allocating smaller folios.

---

Tracking partial dirty is not going to help free up those large
folios, unless the dirty part is migrated to a smaller folio.  Not
sure if it's worth the added complexity, maybe...

---

This is already possible via the /sys/class/bdi interface.

I'm fine with introducing a config option / module option to turn it
off by default.

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that strictlimiting needs to be disabled by default for privileged servers, but expresses uncertainty about Miklos' comfort level with this approach and suggests exploring alternative solutions such as using the /sys/class/bdi interface or a module parameter.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm hoping we're able to keep things as simple as possible by just
getting rid of strictlimiting by default for privileged servers on the
kernel side, without needing any module parameters or libfuse changes,
but I'm not sure if Miklos is comfortable with that. imo if the server
is privileged, it can be trusted to not misbehave and should be given
the same kind of capabilities as a local filesystem. But I guess there
might be the possibility of user regressions if it turns out people's
privileged fuse servers are accidentally buggy.

I think the module parameter makes sense if admins trust unprivileged
servers enough to turn off strictlimiting globally. Otherwise, I think
Miklos just mentioned there's already a /sys/class/bdi interface that
lets admins disable strictlimit on a per-mount basis, so I think it
would be cleaner (though more annoying on the userspace side :)) if
libfuse used this to turn it off by default. The max supported folio
order is set per-inode when the inode is first looked up, not at mount
time, so the admin can disable strictlimit via sysfs after mount
(which is when the bdi sysfs entry gets created) and new inodes will
pick up large folio support from there.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that disabling strictlimiting may not be sufficient for existing large folios and agrees to experiment with an alternative approach.

Author acknowledges that Miklos' feedback about the purpose of writeback throttling is valid and agrees that strictlimiting should not be removed for unprivileged servers.

Author acknowledges that strictlimiting should be disabled for unprivileged servers, and suggests a simple solution: having libfuse disable it on behalf of the privileged server.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think this would help for the case where the writes are to
existing large folios already resident in the page cache, but I can do
some experimentation with this.

---

That's a good point... the whole point of throttling is to cap the
number of pages pinned in RAM...

---

Nice, I think in that case we can just have libfuse disable this on
behalf of the privileged server.

Thanks,
Joanne

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a long-standing bug in the list_lru code that causes the shrinker bit to be set on the wrong cgroup when there is a race between adding an object to the list and deleting the cgroup. The fix involves returning the effective memcg from the locking function, which allows the caller to use it instead of the original memcg. This change ensures that the shrinker bit is set correctly even in the presence of concurrent cgroup deletions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by moving it to a separate static inline function and removing its duplicate definition in the code. The shared function is used in both MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing code duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the alloc_anon_folio() function by flattening its retry loop to improve code readability and maintainability. The original top-level if statement was buried four levels deep, making it difficult to understand and modify. By moving the success path to a more visible location, the code becomes cleaner and easier to work with. This change is made in preparation for additional code that will be added in the next patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change improves handling of cgroups in a suboptimal fashion by avoiding imperfect guard rails and streamlining list operations and reclaim walks. The update also adds calls to create node heads for cgroups on demand when the first object of interest is allocated, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of its current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit(), updating the memcg lock signature, and addressing several other issues such as memory leaks and UAFs. Additionally, the patch series rebases onto the latest mm-unstable and includes various cleanups and improvements to make the conversion safe and minimally invasive.
